mainEntity.name: How much does a Mercedes SLC 180 / 200 engine replacement cost in the UK?

acceptedAnswer.text: A reconditioned SLC 180 engine typically costs £3,000–£5,500 supply only, while an SLC 200 engine costs £3,500–£6,500. Fitting adds £1,500–£2,500 depending on location and workshop rates. Main dealer new engines start from £8,000–£12,000+ supply only. Use our calculator for a figure specific to your car.

mainEntity.name: Is the Mercedes SLC 180 / 200 engine reliable?

acceptedAnswer.text: The M274 is a generally solid and reliable engine. However, it has well-documented weaknesses: timing chain rattle on cold start is the most critical issue, PCV valve failure is a known design defect, thermostat failures are common, and oil leaks can damage the ECU. With proper maintenance, these engines can be reliable, but the timing chain issue is a genuine concern.

mainEntity.name: What are the common problems with the Mercedes SLC 180 / 200?

acceptedAnswer.text: The main known issues are: (1) Timing chain rattle on cold start — the most critical and costly issue, requiring camshaft adjuster replacement, (2) PCV valve failure — a design defect causing fuel smell and potential turbo damage, (3) Thermostat and cooling system failures — causing temperature regulation issues, and (4) Oil leaks from valve cover gaskets and camshaft sensors — can damage the ECU.

mainEntity.name: Is it worth replacing a Mercedes SLC 180 / 200 engine, or should I scrap the car?

acceptedAnswer.text: Depends on vehicle value and condition. Against a well-kept £10,000–£18,000 SLC, a £4,000–£5,500 recon is 20–55% of the car's value. For a clean example with full service history, replacement is often worth it given the SLC's strong retained values. However, if the car has other issues (roof mechanism, electrical faults), evaluate total repair costs before committing.

mainEntity.name: How do I find my Mercedes SLC 180 / 200 engine code?

acceptedAnswer.text: Listed on the VIN plate and vehicle documentation. The SLC 180 uses the M274.910 (1.6L, 156 hp); the SLC 200 uses the M274.920 (2.0L, 184 hp). The engine code is stamped on the cylinder block. Tell us your registration and we'll confirm it for free.

mainEntity.name: Can I prevent timing chain issues on the M274?

acceptedAnswer.text: Prevention is not guaranteed, but you can reduce the risk: (1) Change oil regularly using MB-approved oil — oil quality affects chain and tensioner life, (2) Use premium fuel (95/98 RON), (3) Listen for cold-start rattle — at the first sign, book an inspection immediately, and (4) Check for any outstanding recalls or technical bulletins on your vehicle.

eraMap.title: Mercedes SLC 180 / 200 — Which Engine Does Your Car Actually Have?

rows.eraNote: 1.6L turbo four-cylinder — 156 hp, 250 Nm. Efficient entry-level M274; timing chain tensioner and PCV issues documented
2.0L turbo four-cylinder — 184 hp, 300 Nm. The M274 is generally reliable but has known weaknesses; timing chain rattle on cold start is the key concern

replacementCosts.labourEstimate: Fitting an SLC engine typically takes 12–16 hours at £65–£95/hr (independent) or £110–£160/hr (London/SE dealer). The M274's transverse layout and the SLC's compact engine bay add labour time.

cta.label: → Compare SLC 180 / 200 engine prices from UK specialists

problems.title: Timing Chain Rattle on Cold Start
problems.symptoms: Metallic rattling or clattering noise for 2–3 seconds on cold start, noise that subsides as oil pressure builds, check engine light in later stages

urgency.label: Immediate
urgency.text: if you hear rattling on cold start, book an inspection today

problems.recommendation: This is the most common issue on the M274 engine. The camshaft adjuster sprockets (phasers) wear, causing a metallic rattle on cold start for 2–3 seconds. If left unaddressed, the sprocket can break apart, the chain can jump, and valves will meet pistons — resulting in catastrophic engine failure. Mercedes recommends camshaft adjuster sprocket and chain tensioner replacement as the fix. A £800–£2,000 preventative repair is dramatically cheaper than a £5,000–£8,000 engine replacement. At the first sign of cold-start rattle, book an inspection immediately.

failureLink.label: Read the full timing chain failure guide → /failures/m274-timing-chain/ [NEW FAILURE PAGE NEEDED]

problems.title: PCV Valve Failure (Design Defect)
problems.symptoms: Check engine light, fuel vapour smell in cabin, increased crankcase pressure, potential long-term turbo damage, whistling noise from the engine
problems.replacementCost: £5,000–£8,000 (if ignored — can lead to turbo or engine damage)

urgency.label: Check
urgency.text: book inspection urgently if you notice fuel smell or check engine light

problems.recommendation: The M274 PCV (Positive Crankcase Ventilation) valve has been acknowledged by Mercedes as a "design defect". Failure causes increased crankcase pressure, check engine lights, fuel vapour smell, and potential long-term turbo issues. If you notice a fuel smell in the cabin or a check engine light, have the PCV system inspected immediately. A £300–£800 repair is far cheaper than the turbo or engine damage that can follow if ignored.

failureLink.label: Read the full PCV valve failure guide → /failures/pcv-valve-failure/ [NEW FAILURE PAGE NEEDED]

problems.title: Thermostat & Cooling System Failure
problems.symptoms: Engine running too cool (temperature stuck around 60–70°C), engine overheating, check engine light (P0128), increased fuel consumption
problems.replacementCost: N/A — repair only (does not progress to engine replacement if addressed)

urgency.label: Monitor
urgency.text: book inspection if temperature issues develop

problems.recommendation: The M274's electronic thermostat is a common failure point. If it fails, the engine won't reach proper operating temperature or may overheat, triggering error code P0128. This increases fuel consumption and emissions. If you notice the temperature gauge not reaching normal operating range or erratic cooling, have the thermostat inspected. Replacing the thermostat with an OEM part is essential — aftermarket units often don't work correctly with the ECU.

failureLink.label: Read the full cooling system failure guide

problems.title: Oil Leaks & Carbon Buildup
problems.symptoms: Burning oil smell, oil spots under the car, visible oil residue around the valve covers or camshaft sensors, rough idle, hesitation, misfires
problems.repairCost: £400–£800 (valve cover gasket replacement) / £400–£800 (walnut blasting for carbon buildup)
problems.replacementCost: N/A — repair only (does not progress to engine replacement if addressed)

urgency.label: Monitor
urgency.text: book inspection if you notice oil spots or rough running

problems.recommendation: The M274 suffers from oil leaks through camshaft sensor seals, which can allow oil to travel into the wiring harness and damage the ECU. Valve cover gasket leaks are also common. Preventive measures like oil-stop wiring can help avoid costly repairs. Additionally, the M274's direct injection system causes carbon buildup on intake valves, leading to rough idle and hesitation. Regular valve cleaning and using fuel additives can help maintain performance.

failureLink.label: Read the full oil leak guide → /failures/m274-oil-leaks/ [NEW FAILURE PAGE NEEDED]

buyingChecks.title: Buying This SLC 180 / 200? Check These First
buyIf: * Full service history with regular oil changes using MB-approved oil
* No cold-start rattling — this is a critical warning sign
* Documented timing chain and camshaft adjuster work
* Remainder of manufacturer warranty or extended warranty in place
avoidIf: * Cold-start rattle with no documented timing chain work
* Check engine light with fuel smell — PCV valve failure
* Poor service history — the M274 is a good engine but needs proper maintenance
* Thermostat or cooling system warning codes

repairBuyOrReplace.closingVerdict: The SLC 180 and SLC 200 share the M274 turbocharged four-cylinder engine family — a generally solid and reliable unit that powers a wide range of Mercedes models. However, it has well-documented weaknesses: timing chain rattle on cold start is the most critical and costly issue, PCV valve failure is a known design defect, thermostat failures are common, and oil leaks can damage the ECU. Buy a well-maintained example with full service history and no rattling. Without history? Proceed with caution — the repair bills can be substantial.

cta.label: → Compare SLC 180 / 200 engine prices before you buy

buyingChecklist.intro: Before you buy a used Mercedes SLC 180 or SLC 200 (or before you commit to an engine replacement), check these:
items: * Cold Start — Listen for rattling or clattering for 2–3 seconds (timing chain tensioner / camshaft adjuster warning — this is critical)
* Fuel Smell — Any fuel vapour smell in the cabin? (PCV valve failure)
* Temperature Gauge — Does it reach normal operating temperature? (Thermostat failure)
* Service History — Including the M274 engine and confirmed oil-interval discipline
* Oil Leaks — Look for oil residue around the valve covers and camshaft sensors
* Engine Warning Light — Have codes been read? Look for timing, thermostat or PCV-related codes
* Roof Mechanism — Check the Vario-roof operation; hydraulic leaks and microswitch failures are common
* MOT History — Check for emissions-related failures

engineCodes.matchNote: This table's rows match Section 1B's Era Map exactly.
